# Domotion — using it as a tool (guide for AI agents)

> Domotion (`domotion-svg` on npm) turns HTML/CSS into a **self-contained, animated SVG** by capturing how headless Chromium actually paints it. The output embeds with a plain `<img src="out.svg">`, scales crisply at any size, needs no external assets, and (because text is emitted as glyph `<path>`s) looks identical across browsers. Reach for it to produce marketing/docs demos, UI screenshots, animated diagrams, terminal recordings, or charts as one portable file.

This doc teaches an AI agent (Claude, Cursor, etc.) to **use** Domotion. It is not about editing Domotion's source. When unsure of a flag, run `domotion --help` or `domotion <verb> --help` — that's the source of truth.

## Install

```bash
npm install domotion-svg          # library + the `domotion` CLI (+ svg-to-video, svg-to-image, svg-review, animated-svg-scrubber)
# or one-off:  npx -p domotion-svg domotion capture https://example.com -o out.svg
```

First run auto-installs Playwright's Chromium (`npx playwright install chromium`). It needs a real Chromium — this does not run in a pure-sandbox with no browser.

**Platform note:** output is pixel-faithful to Chromium *on the host OS*. Today only **macOS** is fully calibrated; on Linux/Windows it installs and runs but text rendering isn't yet as faithful (roadmap).

## The CLIs (5 bins)

| CLI | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `domotion` | The main tool: `capture`, `animate`, `template`, `term`, `composite`. |
| `svg-to-video` | Render an animated SVG to MP4/WebM/MOV. |
| `svg-to-image` | Rasterize a still (or one frame of an animated) SVG to PNG/JPEG/PDF/WebP/AVIF/TIFF — your "look at the pixels" tool. |
| `svg-review` | Diff a capture against the Chromium screenshot (for filing fidelity bugs). |
| `animated-svg-scrubber` | Play / scrub / trim / export-frame an animated SVG locally. |

## `domotion capture <input>` — one frame → SVG

`<input>` is a URL, a local `.html` file, `-` for stdin, or a `.har` archive.

```bash
domotion capture https://example.com -o out.svg
domotion capture ./card.html -o card.svg --width 1200 --height 630 --selector ".hero"
cat page.html | domotion capture - -o page.svg
```

Useful flags: `--width N` / `--height N` (viewport, default 800×600) · `--selector <css>` (capture one element, default `body`) · `--clip x,y,w,h` · `--wait-for <css>` (wait for a selector) · `--wait <ms>` · `--color-scheme light|dark` · `--mobile` · `--optimize` (SVGO) · `--chrome phone|browser|window` (wrap in a device bezel, with `--chrome-label`) · `--debug` (write a reproduction bundle for `svg-review`).

**Animated scroll demo** of a long page, in one capture: `--scroll "<pattern>"` captures at successive scroll offsets and composes one scrolling SVG. Patterns: `down:bottom/8s`, `720px,2s until bottom`, `(720px,2s until bottom), (200px,3s)`. Tune with `--scroll-speed <px/s>` and `--scroll-selector <css>` (an inner scroller). See the scroll-pattern docs for the grammar.

## `domotion animate <config.json>` — many frames → one animated SVG

Stitches captured frames with CSS keyframe transitions. The config is JSON validated against `schemas/animate-config.schema.json` (shipped in the package); run `domotion --help` for the full grammar.

```json
{
  "width": 1280, "height": 720, "output": "demo.svg", "optimize": true,
  "frames": [
    { "input": "step1.html", "duration": 1500,
      "transition": { "type": "crossfade", "duration": 300 },
      "animations": [ { "selector": ".bar", "property": "width", "from": "0%", "to": "100%", "duration": 1200 } ],
      "overlays": [ { "kind": "typing", "selector": "input", "text": "hello" } ] },
    { "template": "lower-third", "params": { "title": "Ada Lovelace", "subtitle": "First Programmer" } }
  ]
}
```

Frame kinds: `input` (capture HTML/URL), `template` (embed a built-in template, below), or `cast` (a terminal recording). Transitions: `crossfade`, `cut`, `push-left`, `scroll`, `magic-move`. A frame also supports **intra-frame `animations`** (animate a captured element: `opacity`, `transform`, `translateX/Y`, `scale`, `width`/`height`, `clipPath`), **overlays** (`typing` / `tap` / `svg` / `blink`, optionally anchored to an element), a config-level **`cursor`**, **continuous-session** frames that carry client state across steps (`"continue": true` + DOM-mutation/interaction `actions` + `waitForText`/`waitForGone`/`waitForCount`), and `vars` + `${}` interpolation.

## `domotion template <name>` — parameterized animated SVGs from a few flags

Fastest way to a polished animated SVG without writing HTML. `domotion template list` lists built-ins; `domotion template <name> --help` shows its params.

```bash
domotion template lower-third --title "Ada Lovelace" --subtitle "First Programmer" -o banner.svg
domotion template chart --type donut --data "42,28,18,12" --labels "Search,Direct,Social,Email" -o chart.svg
domotion template kinetic-text --text "Ship it" --variant pop --by char -o title.svg
```

Built-ins: **lower-third** (broadcast banner) · **device-mockup** (wrap a captured page in a phone/browser/window bezel) · **background-loop** (seamless looping background: aurora/orbs/stars/gradient-pan/grid/wave) · **kinetic-text** (animated typography) · **chart** (column/bar/line/pie/donut, single or multi-series) · **chat** (iMessage-style thread) · **subscribe** (follow/subscribe pop-up). Pass scalar params as `--flags`, or arrays/objects via `--params '<json>'`. Third-party templates are npm packages named `domotion-template-<name>` — install one and use it by `<name>`.

## `domotion term --cast <file.cast>` — terminal recording → animated SVG

Record with `asciinema rec demo.cast -c "<command>"`, then `domotion term --cast demo.cast -o demo.svg`. Options for theme, font size, and timing (`domotion term --help`).

**Timing:** the *rendered* play length differs from the recording's wall time (the `*-ms` knobs re-time it). `term` prints it on stderr (`… 13.60s play length …`) — use that number when sizing an `animate` cast frame's `duration`. A blank lead-in *before* the first output renders as zero time. (Synthesizing a `.cast` by hand? Each ANSI sequence needs a real ESC byte — in JSON the `\u001b` escape, which is what `JSON.stringify` of a `"\x1b…"` string produces.)

### Recipe: compose a polished terminal demo

1. **Get a cast.** Record (`asciinema rec`) or synthesize one (asciinema v2: a JSON header line, then `[time,"o",data]` lines; put a real `\u001b` before each SGR).
2. **Find the rendered length.** Run `domotion term --cast x.cast -o x.svg` once and read the `… Ns play length …` it prints.
3. **Compose with intro/outro** via an `animate` config: an `input`/`template` title frame, then the `cast` frame sized to that play length, then an outro. Cast frames are re-synced to start when shown, so multi-frame casts play correctly (not just frame 0):

   ```json
   { "width": 720, "height": 400, "frames": [
     { "template": "kinetic-text", "params": { "text": "Building…" }, "transition": { "type": "crossfade", "duration": 400 } },
     { "cast": "./build.cast", "term": { "theme": "dark" }, "duration": 13600 },
     { "input": "./done.html", "duration": 2000 } ] }
   ```
4. **Transparent canvas** so the result floats on any host background: give the cast theme a transparent `bg` (or omit a page background on an `input` frame) — a transparent root yields a transparent SVG.
5. **Window chrome / desktop.** To wrap the *animated* terminal in window chrome — or place the framed window on a desktop — use `domotion composite` (below): a `cast` layer with `"chrome": {"device":"window"}` keeps the terminal animating inside the bezel. (`device-mockup` also takes a `screenSvg` to nest an animated screen.)

## `domotion composite <config.json>` — layer animated SVGs into one (nested compositing)

Stacks **layers** — each a `cast`, a `template`, or a pre-rendered `svg` (any of which may be **animated**) — into one self-contained animated SVG, each **placed** and on its **own timeline**, with animation preserved. This is how you nest one animated thing inside another (a terminal window on a desktop; a scrolling site inside a browser bezel). Layers can carry a device `chrome` wrapper and **layer-level animations** (move / scale / fade) — e.g. a window that resizes mid-playback.

```json
{ "width": 1100, "height": 720, "output": "desktop.svg", "background": "#000", "duration": 13600,
  "layers": [
    { "svg": "desktop.svg", "x": 0, "y": 0, "width": 1100, "height": 720 },
    { "cast": "build.cast", "term": { "theme": "dark" }, "chrome": { "device": "window", "label": "build" },
      "x": 150, "y": 90,
      "animations": [ { "property": "scale", "from": 1, "to": 1.28, "start": 6000, "duration": 800, "transformOrigin": "0 0" } ] }
  ] }
```

Per layer: one source (`svg` / `cast` / `template`), placement (`x`/`y`/`width`/`height`/`clip`), timeline (`start`, `mode: hold|stretch|loop`, `duration`), optional `chrome`, and `animations`. Layer animations: `translateX`/`translateY`/`scale`/`transform`/`opacity`, plus `clipScaleX`/`clipScaleY` to **resize a layer's box** (e.g. shrink a window so its content reflows while its buttons keep size) — not scale its contents. Programmatic equivalent: `composeAnimatedLayers(layers, opts)` (named export). See the runnable `examples/composite/` + `examples/composite-desktop.ts` (a desktop with a terminal window that resizes mid-run, reflowing the terminal) and `docs/77-nested-animated-compositing.md`.

## `svg-to-video <input.svg> -o out.mp4` — animated SVG → video

```bash
svg-to-video demo.svg -o demo.mp4 --width 1280
svg-to-video demo.svg -o demo.webm --format vp9 --fps 60 --music bed.mp3
```

`--width`/`--height` (contain, aspect-preserving), `--fps` (default 30), `--duration <s>` (default: one animation loop), `--music <file>`.

## `svg-to-image <input.svg> -o out.png` — SVG → still image (PNG/JPEG/PDF/WebP/AVIF/TIFF)

The one-shot rasterizer for *looking at* what you produced (see "Work the loop" below). Output format follows the `-o` extension: `.png` / `.webp` / `.avif` / `.tiff` (keep alpha for transparent SVGs; webp/avif take `--quality`), `.jpg`/`.jpeg` (`--quality 1-100`), or `.pdf` (a single vector page sized to the SVG). WebP/AVIF/TIFF are transcoded via the bundled `sharp` (no extra install).

```bash
svg-to-image card.svg -o card.png                 # still → PNG at intrinsic size
svg-to-image card.svg -o card@2x.png --scale 2    # crisp retina (2×) raster
svg-to-image demo.svg -o payoff.png --at 4000     # one frame of an animation, at 4s
svg-to-image poster.svg -o poster.pdf             # vector PDF
```

`--at <ms>` (timeline position for an animated SVG; default 0) · `--width`/`--height` (contain, aspect-preserving; default intrinsic) · `--scale <n>` (raster supersample, default 1) · `--background <css>` (default transparent) · `--quality <1-100>` (JPEG). Input is an SVG file only — for URL/HTML → image, `domotion capture … -o x.svg` first.

## Programmatic API (`import … from "domotion-svg"`)

For Node scripts instead of the CLI: `captureElementTree()` + `elementTreeToSvg()` (capture → SVG), `generateAnimatedSvg()` (compose frames), `composeAnimatedLayers()` (nest/layer animated SVGs — see `composite` above), `renderTemplateToSvg(template, params, { browser })` (render a template), `launchChromium()` (browser lifecycle), `optimizeSvg()`, `wrapInDeviceChrome()`. All are named exports of `domotion-svg`. See `examples/` in the repo and the API docs.

## Making it compelling — a design playbook for agents

> Domotion faithfully renders whatever HTML/CSS and timing you give it. Whether the result is *good* is on you. When a prompt or ticket asks for an animation or a still, don't just make it work — make a deliberate design decision at each step below. The principles here are distilled from established practice (Disney's 12 principles of animation, Material Design / Apple HIG motion specs, WCAG contrast, Tufte / *Storytelling with Data*) and mapped to Domotion's actual levers. Default to **restraint**: every motion, color, and element must earn its place.

### Work the loop — build → render → *look* → critique → iterate

You cannot judge a visual you haven't seen. **Never ship the first render.** The output is an ordinary, self-contained, standards-compliant SVG, so you can look at it cheaply:

1. **Render**, then **rasterize and actually view it as pixels.** For a still: `svg-to-image out.svg -o out.png` (the published one-shot rasterizer) and *look at the PNG*. For an animation: pull key beats as stills with `svg-to-image out.svg -o payoff.png --at <ms>` (the hook frame, the payoff frame, any frame you suspect is broken), and/or `svg-to-video out.svg -o out.mp4` to watch it; `animated-svg-scrubber out.svg` is the interactive timeline bench. `svg-review` is for pixel-diffing a capture against Chromium (fidelity bugs), not aesthetic review.
2. **Critique against the checklist** (bottom of this section): hierarchy, contrast, easing, pacing, restraint. Name the single weakest thing.
3. **Fix that one thing and re-render.** Iterate.
4. **Prototype cheap, commit late.** Get the *still* frames right first (composition, color, type) — they're fast to render and most problems are visible without motion. Add motion only once the frames look good; motion can't rescue a weak frame.
5. **Iterate visibly when working with a human.** Show intermediate renders and ask before going further on a fork (format, palette direction, motion style) — a 5-second still is a cheap way to converge.

### Motion — timing, easing, and the cut

**Principle: motion should feel like it has weight and intent. The easing curve is the "adverb"; almost never leave it `linear`.**

- **Override the easing.** Intra-frame `animations` default to **`linear`, which looks robotic** — set `easing` on every one (also available on `transition`, overlay enter/exit, and composite-layer animations; all accept `cubic-bezier(...)`):
  - **Entrances / things appearing → ease-out** (fast start, soft settle): `cubic-bezier(0.215,0.61,0.355,1)`.
  - **Exits / things leaving → ease-in** (accelerate away): `cubic-bezier(0.55,0.055,0.675,0.19)`.
  - **On-screen moves / resizes (start and end at rest) → ease-in-out**: `cubic-bezier(0.645,0.045,0.355,1)`, or Material's standard `cubic-bezier(0.2,0,0,1)`.
  - **Emphasis / a playful "pop"** (a badge, a success state) → overshoot: `cubic-bezier(0.175,0.885,0.32,1.275)`. Use sparingly.
  - **Continuous/ambient loops only** (spinner, marquee, background drift) → `linear`. This is the one correct use of linear.
- **Durations** (`duration` in ms): micro-feedback ~100 ms · default enter/transition **250–300 ms** · larger/hero move 350–450 ms · make **exits ~20–30% shorter** than their entrance. Floors/ceilings: under ~100 ms reads as broken, over ~500 ms drags. Scale duration up with travel distance to keep *perceived* speed constant.
- **Hold long enough to read.** A frame's `duration` is its on-screen time — budget **reading time**, not a fixed beat: ≥~0.8–1 s for a glanceable state, longer for a sentence the viewer must read. A frame that flashes by is wasted.
- **One focal motion at a time; sequence the rest.** Don't animate many things simultaneously. Stagger siblings with an increasing `delay` (~**30–60 ms** apart — e.g. list items at delay 0, 50, 100…) so attention cascades instead of scattering. Keep a whole cascade under ~1–2 s.
- **Pick the transition for what the cut means** (cinematic grammar):
  - `cut` = hard cut, energetic, "new beat now" — the neutral default between distinct scenes.
  - `crossfade` = cross-dissolve, soft / passage of time — for loosely related states.
  - `push-left` = a pan, spatial progression — "next in a sequence."
  - `scroll` = a reveal — uncover a long page/region.
  - `magic-move` = a **match cut** — the *same* scene rearranged (a card grows, a list item relocates, a logo moves between slides). It diffs the two frames and slides matched elements, so it reads as one continuous space. Reach for it for before→after where layout persists; pair elements explicitly with `data-magic-key`.
- **Combine effects by nesting, not stacking.** Only **one animation per element** — to move *and* fade, put the move on a wrapper and the fade on an inner element. To scale/rotate about an element's own center, set `transformOrigin: "center"` (SVG transforms otherwise pivot about the origin).
- **Seamless loops:** for ambient/looping pieces use `repeat: "infinite"` + **`alternate: true`** (the ping-pong hides the seam), or make the last frame's state identical to the first. A mismatched first/last frame produces a visible stutter.
- **Stay cross-browser-safe and accessible.** Animate only `opacity` / `transform` / `translate` / `scale` / `clipPath` (see Gotchas — animated `filter`/`blur` is Chromium-only inside an `<img>` SVG). The multi-frame `animate` pipeline **already emits a `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` block** that pins motion for users who ask for less — don't fight it; lean on it.

### Stills & frames — color, composition, type

**These apply to the HTML/CSS you author for `capture` inputs and template HTML.** Domotion captures real Chromium paint, so good CSS *is* the lever — design the page well and the SVG inherits it.

- **Color — look intentional, not random.** Build on a neutral base + **one** accent reserved for the single most important thing (the CTA, the key number). Follow **60-30-10** (60% dominant/neutral, 30% secondary, 10% accent). Pick a harmony on purpose (analogous = calm; complementary/split-complementary = punchy). Meet **WCAG contrast: 4.5:1** for body text, **3:1** for large text and UI/graphic marks — check it, don't eyeball it. Dark mode ≠ inverted: use a near-black surface (`#121212`), desaturated accents, off-white (not pure-white) text; pass `--color-scheme dark` so UA defaults match.
- **Composition — one focal point, lead the eye.** Establish a single focal element via size/weight/contrast or **isolation** (whitespace around it). Start with *too much* whitespace and remove; keep **space between groups > space within a group** or grouping collapses. Align everything to a grid (8-px spacing scale: 4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64…). Place key elements off-center (rule of thirds); for marketing heroes the eye runs a Z (logo top-left → visual → CTA bottom-right). Body text measure **45–75 characters**; left-align body (center only short headings).
- **Typography — designed, not defaulted.** One **modular scale** (base 16 px × ~1.25 → 16/20/25/31/39/49…); **1–2 typefaces**, **2 weights** (e.g. 500 body / 700 emphasis), **2 text colors** (dark + muted gray). Hierarchy from size + weight + color *together*, never size alone, and never de-emphasize with a thinner weight. Body line-height ~1.5, headings ~1.15; tighten tracking on large display text, loosen it on ALL-CAPS labels.

### Data & charts

The built-in `chart` template (`column`/`bar`/`line`/`pie`/`donut`) makes the chart; you still make the *choices*:

- **Pick by the question:** comparison/ranking → bar/column; change over time → line; part-to-whole → pie/donut *only* with **≤5 slices** summing to 100% (otherwise a bar is more accurate — prefer it).
- **Bars must start at zero** (length encodes value; a truncated axis lies). Line charts may use a non-zero baseline if labeled honestly.
- **Sort categories by value** (unless time/natural order), **label directly** instead of relying on a legend, and **emphasize one series** by coloring it the accent while the rest stay gray. Cap categorical palettes at ~6 colors. Strip chartjunk — no 3D, no heavy gridlines.

### Story — a demo is a tiny three-act

**Principle: even a 10–20 s clip is hook → build → payoff, one idea per scene.**

- **Open with a hook** in the first ~1–3 s — the end-state the viewer wants, or the question the demo answers.
- **One idea per frame.** Don't stack concepts into one beat; give each its own frame and hold.
- **Show, don't tell** — let the result *be* the proof (the diff resolves, the chart fills, the build succeeds), rather than a label asserting it.
- **Vary the rhythm** and put a brief hold just before the payoff so it lands. Frame the piece with a quick intro (`kinetic-text` / a title `template`) and end cleanly on the payoff (don't trail off mid-action).
- **Terminal demos:** size the `cast` frame's `duration` to the play length `term` prints on stderr; wrap with an intro/outro via `animate`, or place the window on a desktop via `composite`.

### Consistency at scale — lock a house style

When a project needs many on-brand visuals, define the style **once** and reuse it (these are design tokens in practice — color/type/spacing decided once, referenced everywhere):

- **Author a project template package** — `domotion-template-<name>` (see the authoring section + `examples/template-package/`). Bake the brand's palette, type scale, spacing, and motion vocabulary into the template; expose only the content as params. Then every banner/card/chart is `domotion template <name> --title …` and is consistent by construction. This is the strongest consistency mechanism.
- **Share a brand stylesheet.** Put palette/type/spacing in one `brand.css` and `<link>`/inline it into every `capture` input's HTML, so all stills draw from the same tokens. Capture at a **fixed `--width`/`--height`** per format (e.g. 1200×630 social, 1080×1080 square) for a uniform set.
- **Parameterize configs with `vars`.** Hoist brand values (colors, copy, URLs, file paths) into the top-level `vars` map and `${}`-interpolate them across frames, so one config retargets without edits.
- **Reuse framing:** the same `--chrome phone|browser|window` bezel and `--color-scheme` across a set keeps device mockups and screenshots visually uniform.

### Pre-ship checklist

Before returning a result, verify:

- [ ] **Hierarchy:** one clear focal point; the most important thing is the most prominent.
- [ ] **Contrast:** text/marks meet 4.5:1 (body) / 3:1 (large & UI).
- [ ] **Color:** a deliberate palette, one accent, 60-30-10 — not default/random colors.
- [ ] **Type:** one scale, ≤2 fonts, ≤2 weights; comfortable measure & line-height.
- [ ] **Spacing:** consistent scale; groups separated by whitespace.
- [ ] **Easing:** set on every animation (not `linear`, except ambient loops); entrances ease-out, exits ease-in.
- [ ] **Pacing:** frames held long enough to read; one focal motion at a time; staggered where appropriate.
- [ ] **Story:** reads as hook → build → payoff; one idea per frame.
- [ ] **Restraint:** removed any motion/decoration that doesn't carry meaning.
- [ ] **Cross-browser-safe** props only; loop (if any) has no visible seam.
- [ ] **You looked at the rendered pixels** (still PNG and/or video), not just the config.

### Cheat-sheet

- **Durations (ms):** micro 100 · default enter/transition 250–300 · large/hero 350–450 · exit = entrance −20–30% · hold a readable frame ≥800–1500 (+ reading time) · stagger 30–60 between siblings.
- **Easing:** enter `cubic-bezier(0.215,0.61,0.355,1)` (ease-out) · exit `cubic-bezier(0.55,0.055,0.675,0.19)` (ease-in) · move `cubic-bezier(0.645,0.045,0.355,1)` (ease-in-out) · pop `cubic-bezier(0.175,0.885,0.32,1.275)` (overshoot) · loops `linear` only.
- **Color:** neutral base + 1 accent · 60-30-10 · contrast 4.5:1 body / 3:1 large & UI · dark-mode surface `#121212`, off-white text.
- **Type:** 16 px base × 1.25 → 16/20/25/31/39/49 · 1–2 fonts · 2 weights · body line-height 1.5 / headings 1.15 · measure 45–75 chars.
- **Spacing (px):** 4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64, 96 (8-pt grid). Inner spacing ≤ outer spacing.
- **Charts:** right chart for the question · bars start at 0 · pie ≤5 slices · sort by value · gray + 1 accent · direct labels.

## Output characteristics

- One **self-contained** `<svg>` document — no external fonts/images/scripts. Embed with `<img src="out.svg">` (CSS animations play, it's lazy-loadable and accessible with `alt`), or inline it if you need CSS to reach in.
- **Scales** crisply at any size; **text is real glyph paths**, so it renders identically across browsers.
- Some content is embedded as a raster `<image>` rather than native SVG (color emoji, vertical text, `<canvas>`/`<iframe>`/`<video>`, conic gradients, `<textarea>` content) — still self-contained.
- **Transparent background round-trips.** A transparent page/root background (or a transparent terminal theme `bg`) yields a **transparent SVG** — no opaque backdrop rect is painted — so the output composites over whatever host background it lands on. Give the root an opaque background if you want one baked in.

## Gotchas / constraints

- **Needs Chromium** (Playwright). CI: pre-install with `npx playwright install chromium` to keep the first run fast.
- **Cross-browser-safe animation only.** Animate `opacity` / `transform` / `translate` / `scale` / `clipPath`. Do **not** rely on animated CSS `filter` (e.g. `blur()`) — it renders in Chromium but not Safari/others inside an `<img>`-embedded SVG.
- **One intra-frame animation per element** — to move *and* fade an element, wrap it (move on the wrapper, fade on the inner). For a transform to pivot about an element's own box (e.g. a center scale), set `transformOrigin` on the animation.
- Output reflects Chromium-on-the-capturing-OS; verify text fidelity on macOS until Linux/Windows are calibrated.
- **Nesting animation:** `cast` / `template` / `scroll` frames (and a frame's intra-frame `animations`) **keep** their animation, as do **`composite` layers** (below). An `svg` **overlay** renders its input as a **static snapshot**. For a general animated-inside-animated composite (a terminal window on a desktop, a scrolling site inside a browser bezel), use `domotion composite`.
- **"glyph helper unavailable — using fontkit" is harmless.** That stderr line is a **performance-only** fallback; the SVG output is identical either way. Pre-warm with `acquireGlyphHelper()` or `DOMOTION_HELPER_PATH` only if you want the faster path.

## Going deeper

- `domotion --help` and `domotion <verb> --help` — authoritative flag reference.
- Hosted docs + demo gallery: https://brianwestphal.github.io/domotion/
- `FEATURES.md` (shipped) — per-feature support checklist.
- Issues / fidelity bugs: capture with `--debug`, review with `svg-review`, file at https://github.com/brianwestphal/domotion/issues
